epithets.gloss: Virgil's signature epithet, used some twenty times across the poem. *Pius* is untranslatable in English: it fuses duty to the gods, duty to parents, duty to country, and duty to the unborn future into one word. Dryden reaches most often for "pious" but also "good," "the pious chief," or "the good Aeneas." The formulaic first-occurrence marker signals the Virgilian system Aeneas lives inside — he is not simply a man named Aeneas, he is always *pius* Aeneas, as Achilles is always *swift-footed* in Homer.
epithets.subject: Aeneas

1.context: The dart aloft, and clench the pointed spear!” / Thus while the pious prince his fate bewails, / Fierce Boreas drove against his flying sails,

4.context: Involv’d in clouds, and vanish’d out of sight. / The pious prince was seiz’d with sudden fear; / Mute was his tongue, and upright stood his hair.

5.context: Within the hollow tomb retir’d to rest. / The pious prince, surpris’d at what he view’d, / The fun’ral honours with more zeal renew’d,

6.context: Thus, while their sev’ral charges they fulfil, / The pious prince ascends the sacred hill / Where Phoebus is ador’d; and seeks the shade

7.context: Here warlike Latins hold the happy lands. / The pious chief, who sought by peaceful ways / To found his empire, and his town to raise,

10.context: He cast to heav’n, on Argos thinks, and dies. / The pious Trojan then his jav’lin sent; / The shield gave way; thro’ treble plates it went

11.context: Above the waves, and left her wat’ry bed; / The pious chief, whom double cares attend / For his unburied soldiers and his friend,

12.context: Thus while he spoke, unmindful of defence, / A winged arrow struck the pious prince. / But, whether from some human hand it came,

epithets.gloss: "Father Aeneas" — used only after he becomes paterfamilias of the Trojan remnant. Dryden renders as "the father" or "father Aeneas." Its appearance marks the moment Aeneas ceases to be a young warrior and becomes the ancestor Virgil is building toward.
epithets.subject: Aeneas

epithets.gloss: The tragic formula — "unhappy," "ill-fated." *Infelix* is the same word Virgil uses elsewhere for the doomed or marked-by-death; it is Dido's name on the poem's lips from the moment she falls in love. Dryden spreads the sense across "unhappy," "unhappy queen," "unhappy Dido," and "the hapless queen."
epithets.subject: Dido

1.context: With greedy pleasure, and devour’d his charms. / Unhappy Dido little thought what guest, / How dire a god, she drew so near her breast;

2.context: These eyes beheld him when he march’d between / The brother kings: I saw th’ unhappy queen, / The hundred wives, and where old Priam stood,

4.context: All else of nature’s common gift partake: / Unhappy Dido was alone awake. / Nor sleep nor ease the furious queen can find;

6.context: And, as his love inspir’d him, thus he said: / “Unhappy queen! then is the common breath / Of rumour true, in your reported death,

7.context: “Io, ye Latian dames! if any here / Hold your unhappy queen, Amata, dear; / If there be here,” she said, “who dare maintain

epithets.gloss: Book I, line 4: "because of cruel Juno's unforgetting wrath." The phrase that opens the poem's plot. Dryden: "haughty Juno's unrelenting hate." Juno's *memor* (remembering) anger drives the whole Trojan war-in-Italy and is the largest structural fact of the poem.
epithets.subject: Juno

1.context: Arms, and the man I sing, who, forc’d by fate, / And haughty Juno’s unrelenting hate, / Expell’d and exil’d, left the Trojan shore.

8.context: Fear not! The war shall end in lasting peace, / And all the rage of haughty Juno cease. / And that this nightly vision may not seem

10.context: Which may my wand’ring fugitives receive; / (Since haughty Juno will not give you leave;) / Then, father, (if I still may use that name,)

epithets.gloss: "Gracious / nourishing Venus" — Venus in her maternal aspect, as the mother of Aeneas and through him the mother of Rome. Dryden: "the gracious queen," "the goddess-mother."
epithets.subject: Venus

1.context: Lo! in the deep recesses of the wood, / Before his eyes his goddess mother stood: / A huntress in her habit and her mien;

2.context: And then the sire himself to the dire altar drew. / O goddess mother, give me back to Fate; / Your gift was undesir’d, and came too late!

8.context: Is giv’n of promis’d aid, and arms divine. / My goddess mother, whose indulgent care / Foresaw the dangers of the growing war,

12.context: And the blood follows, where the weapon flies. / His goddess mother is not near, to shroud / The flying coward with an empty cloud.”

epithets.gloss: "Loyal Achates" — *fidelitas* personified. Achates is Aeneas's companion throughout; his epithet is so durable it entered English as a figure for a faithful friend ("a true Achates"). Dryden: "faithful" or "trusty."
epithets.subject: Achates

similes.opener: As when some peasant, in a bushy brake,
similes.resolver: So from our arms surpris’d Androgeos flies.
similes.preview: As when some peasant, in a bushy brake, Has with unwary footing press’d a snake; He starts aside, astonish’d, when he spies His rising crest, blue neck, and rolling eyes; So from our arms surpris’d Androgeos flies.

similes.opener: As when you stemm’d the strong Malean flood,
similes.preview: As when you stemm’d the strong Malean flood, And o’er the Syrtes’ broken billows row’d. I seek not now the foremost palm to gain; Though yet—But, ah! that haughty wish is vain!

similes.opener: As when a snake, surpris’d upon the road,
similes.resolver: So slowly to the port the Centaur tends,
similes.preview: As when a snake, surpris’d upon the road, Is crush’d athwart her body by the load Of heavy wheels; or with a mortal wound Her belly bruis’d, and trodden to the ground: In vain, with loosen’d curls, she crawls along; Yet, fierce above, she b

similes.opener: As when his thund’ring sword and pointed spear
similes.preview: As when his thund’ring sword and pointed spear Drove headlong to their ships, and glean’d the routed rear. They rais’d a feeble cry, with trembling notes; But the weak voice deceiv’d their gasping throats.

similes.opener: So, when with crackling flames a cauldron fries,
similes.preview: So, when with crackling flames a cauldron fries, The bubbling waters from the bottom rise: Above the brims they force their fiery way; Black vapours climb aloft, and cloud the day.

similes.opener: Like a long team of snowy swans on high,
similes.preview: Like a long team of snowy swans on high, Which clap their wings, and cleave the liquid sky, When, homeward from their wat’ry pastures borne, They sing, and Asia’s lakes their notes return.

similes.opener: So, when the sun by day, or moon by night,
similes.preview: So, when the sun by day, or moon by night, Strike on the polish’d brass their trembling light, The glitt’ring species here and there divide, And cast their dubious beams from side to side;

similes.opener: So, from the seas, exerts his radiant head
similes.preview: So, from the seas, exerts his radiant head The star by whom the lights of heav’n are led; Shakes from his rosy locks the pearly dews, Dispels the darkness, and the day renews.

similes.opener: Like a fair flow’r by the keen share oppress’d;
similes.preview: Like a fair flow’r by the keen share oppress’d; Like a white poppy sinking on the plain, Whose heavy head is overcharg’d with rain. Despair, and rage, and vengeance justly vow’d,

similes.opener: Like a fierce tiger pent amid the fold.
similes.preview: Like a fierce tiger pent amid the fold. Too late his blazing buckler they descry, And sparkling fires that shot from either eye, His mighty members, and his ample breast,

similes.opener: As when a whirlwind, rushing to the shore
similes.preview: As when a whirlwind, rushing to the shore From the mid ocean, drives the waves before; The painful hind with heavy heart foresees The flatted fields, and slaughter of the trees;

similes.opener: As the black swallow near the palace plies;
similes.resolver: So drives the rapid goddess o’er the plains;
similes.preview: As the black swallow near the palace plies; O’er empty courts, and under arches, flies; Now hawks aloft, now skims along the flood, To furnish her loquacious nest with food: So drives the rapid goddess o’er the plains;

similes.resolver: So Turnus, hasting headlong to the town,

similes.opener: As when two bulls for their fair female fight
similes.resolver: Such was the combat in the listed ground;
similes.preview: As when two bulls for their fair female fight In Sila’s shades, or on Taburnus’ height; With horns adverse they meet; the keeper flies; Mute stands the herd; the heifers roll their eyes, And wait th’ event; which victor they shall bear, And

apostrophes.anchorText: And thou, O matron of immortal fame,
apostrophes.title: Apostrophe to Caieta
apostrophes.latinLine: Tū quoque lītoribus nostrīs, Aenēia nūtrīx, / aeternam moriēns fāmam, Caiēta, dedistī.
apostrophes.description: The opening lines of Book VII. Virgil turns from his narrative to address Caieta, Aeneas's nurse, whose death has just given a name to the place on the Italian coast where she was buried. The gesture is typically Virgilian — the poet stepping forward to confer immortality on a character the plot would otherwise pass over.

apostrophes.anchorText: A new Marcellus shall arise in thee!
apostrophes.title: Tu Marcellus eris
apostrophes.latinLine: Heu, miserande puer, sī quā fāta aspera rumpās, / tū Marcellus eris.
apostrophes.description: The most famous apostrophe in Latin poetry. In Elysium, Anchises shows Aeneas the shade of a beautiful youth who will not live — Marcellus, Augustus's heir, dead in 23 BC. Virgil, addressing the unborn dead boy directly, promises him immortality through the verse the reader is reading. Servius records that when Virgil recited this passage to Octavia (Marcellus's mother), she fainted.

apostrophes.anchorText: O happy friends! for, if my verse can give
apostrophes.title: Fortunati ambo
apostrophes.latinLine: Fortūnātī ambō! Sī quid mea carmina possunt, / nūlla diēs umquam memorī vōs eximet aevō.
apostrophes.description: After the night-raid of Nisus and Euryalus ends in their deaths, Virgil addresses the two dead youths directly: "Happy both! If my songs have any power, no day will ever erase you from mindful time." The apostrophe is the poem consciously promising what it will deliver — and has delivered, for two thousand years.

apostrophes.anchorText: And here, heroic youth, ’tis here I must
apostrophes.title: Apostrophe to Lausus
apostrophes.latinTag: Hīc mortis dūrae cāsum tuaque optima facta…
apostrophes.latinLine: Hīc mortis dūrae cāsum tuaque optima facta, / sī qua fidem tantō est operī lātūra vetustās, / nōn equidem nec tē, iuvenis memorande, silēbō.
apostrophes.description: At the death of Lausus, son of Mezentius, Virgil breaks his own third-person narrative to promise the young warrior — killed defending his tyrant father — that he will not be silenced by the poet. The pattern is the same as Nisus-Euryalus: apostrophe as the moral judgment the narration refuses to make in its own voice.

apostrophes.anchorText: Of polish’d ivory this, that of transparent horn:
apostrophes.title: The Two Gates of Sleep
apostrophes.latinLine: Sunt geminae Somnī portae, quārum altera fertur / cornea, quā vēris facilis datur exitus umbrīs…
apostrophes.description: Book VI ends with Aeneas and the Sibyl leaving the underworld through the Gate of Ivory — the gate, Virgil says, through which false dreams are sent up to the living. Scholars have debated for two thousand years why Virgil sends his hero out through the gate of falsehood. Servius was already puzzled in the fourth century. The annotation elsewhere in this apparatus lays out the honest state of the debate without resolving it.
